executive_summary.opening_paragraph: This document is the output of our discovery process. Over the past 60 days we have gathered financial, legal, tax, and operational data on you and your business, met with you both, reviewed three years of returns and statements, and analyzed the operating agreement, ownership structure, and current insurance program for Holloway Industrial Solutions. What follows is our recommended path forward — both immediate priorities and the longer-term strategy that protects what you have built and accelerates what you are building.
executive_summary.two_themes_paragraph: Two themes shape this plan. First, HIS is approaching a value range ($32M–$48M) where the cost of inaction on estate, entity, and tax planning compounds rapidly — the wills date from 2014 when net worth was roughly $3M, the Kennesaw facility sits inside the operating LLC, and there is no holdco, no ILIT, no funded buy/sell, and no PTET election. Second, you have unsolicited inbound interest from both a strategic (regional MEP roll-up) and a financial buyer (lower-middle-market PE) — which means a transaction window of three to five years is realistic and possibly shorter, and the planning we do now determines how much of the value you keep.
executive_summary.what_this_means_closer: Acted on together, the five priorities above remove the largest catastrophic exposures on your personal balance sheet (umbrella, cyber, estate liquidity), build the receiving architecture (children's trusts, ILIT) that the rest of the transfer planning needs in order to land somewhere outside your taxable estate, and begin the annual exclusion gifting program that compounds quietly in the background. They are deliberately the foundation, not the headline tax wins — those come next, in Sections 1 through 4 below, where the entity restructure, the GRAT, the IDGT sale, the SLAT, and the GST/dynasty allocation produce the eight- and nine-figure transfers that this foundation makes possible. Sequenced correctly over the next 24 months, the combined impact funds professional fees many times over and positions you for a transaction in 3–5 years from a far stronger structural starting point.

our_process.intro_paragraph: Financial planning is not a one-time event. It is a relationship that runs alongside your business, your family, and the decisions you will make over the next decade. This document is one milestone in that relationship — the formal handoff from discovery to implementation — but the work continues from here.

stages.name: Discovery (completed)
stages.body: Over the past 60 days we have gathered three years of business and personal tax returns, the HIS operating agreement, the 2014 wills, insurance summaries, mortgage statements, and the client-prepared balance sheet. We met with both of you, walked through goals and concerns, mapped the existing partner roster, and identified the gaps (estate attorney, M&A counsel, valuation/Q-of-E, specialty tax credits firm). We also formed a working view on the two unsolicited buyer approaches and the realistic transaction window. That work is the foundation of everything that follows.

stages.name: Plan delivery (today)
stages.body: This document is the deliverable. It is organized so that the Executive Summary gives you the 90-second readout, the Findings & Observations section explains where you stand today, and the Recommendations sections — Business first, Personal second — lay out specific guidance with named partners, dollar ranges, and timing windows. The Implementation Roadmap is your project plan; the Decisions Needed page is what we will discuss at our next meeting.

stages.name: Implementation
stages.body: Implementation begins immediately after the delivery meeting. PSA quarterbacks the partner team — we already have introductory calls scheduled with the estate attorney and CPA candidates we are recommending, and we will coordinate with M&A counsel, the valuation/Q-of-E firm, the specialty R&D credits firm, and the insurance broker as each section's work activates. You should expect a 30-day implementation check-in after delivery, then bi-weekly cadence through the first 120 days as the foundational entity, estate, and risk work executes.

stages.name: Ongoing review
stages.body: Once the foundation is in place, the relationship moves to the standing cadence outlined in the Meeting Cadence section: quarterly tax check-ins, semi-annual investment reviews, an annual full-plan review, and triggered reviews around any material business event (LOI, valuation refresh, key-person change, regulatory change). The plan is a living document — it gets refreshed each year against actual results, the §7520 rate environment, OBBBA developments, and your evolving transaction timeline.

our_process.how_to_read_paragraph: The Executive Summary is your 90-second readout. The Findings & Observations section explains where you stand today — strengths first, then opportunities organized by category. The Recommendations sections are our specific guidance — Business first (Sections 1–7), Personal second (Sections 8–12). Each recommendation opens with a strategic frame, then bulleted action items with the partner who executes, the dollar exposure, and the timing. The Implementation Roadmap is your project plan in table form. The Decisions Needed page is what we will discuss at our next meeting. The Glossary at the back defines every technical term we use.

findings_observations.intro_paragraph: Based on what we have reviewed, here is where you stand today — what is working, and where we see opportunities to strengthen your position before a transaction window opens. We lead with strengths because they matter: most of what makes the next five years possible is already built. The opportunities are addressable, and every one of them is taken up in the Recommendations sections that follow.

strengths.body: Profitable, fast-growing business with a defensible niche. HIS is running a 22.7% three-year revenue CAGR (from $28.4M in 2022 to $42.8M in 2024) at expanding EBITDA margins (13.0% → 14.0% → 15.0%), in verticals — data center MEP, healthcare, advanced manufacturing — where demand is structurally accelerating, not cyclical. The $48M backlog (~14 months) is a real moat.

Strong operating partner. Derek Chen is a vested 12% owner who is genuinely capable of running the business, which is what gives a future transaction its optionality. Most owner-led specialty contractors at this scale do not have this — buyers will pay for it.

Both spouses are excellent insurance risks. Marcus and Catherine are both insurable at preferred non-smoker rates, which is unusual and time-sensitive. Insurability degrades with age and any health change; the planning we recommend below uses this window deliberately.

Existing qualified retirement plan in place. Marcus is at the 401(k) deferral cap with catch-up. We are optimizing and adding layers (cash-balance evaluation, profit-sharing, Roth/after-tax mechanics), not starting from scratch.

Strong banking relationship and clean credit posture. The Truist relationship supports a $4M revolving LOC, the East Cobb mortgage is locked at 3.10%, and there is no consumer or personal direct debt outside the residence. That is a clean platform to plan from.

Engaged ownership willing to invest in long-term planning. Both of you have made time for this process, brought thoughtful goals (Derek's continuity, Catherine's real-estate vision, the children's age-35 rule, the trade scholarships), and are open to changing what needs to change. That is itself rare and is the single biggest predictor of whether a plan like this gets executed.

bullets: The Kennesaw headquarters / fabrication facility ($4.2M) is held inside the operating LLC. This exposes the property to operating-business liability and forfeits multiple tax-planning opportunities (cost segregation, §469 grouping, separate appreciation tracking, clean stock-vs-asset transaction structuring).

No holding-company layer above the operating entity. Limits flexibility for an F-reorganization, charitable gifting of operating-company stock, voting/non-voting recapitalization, and basis planning ahead of a transaction.

HIS operating agreement dates from formation (2009) and does not reflect Derek's vested 12%, current valuation realities, transfer restrictions, or modern buy/sell mechanics. Referenced buy/sell document may not actually exist as a standalone instrument.

Wills date from 2014, when net worth was approximately $3M. No revocable trust, no ILIT, no DPOA, no healthcare directive, no HIPAA authorization, no beneficiary review since 2014, no guardian named for Sophie (born after the wills were executed).
Olivia turned 19 with no HIPAA authorization, healthcare POA, or financial POA — meaning if she is hospitalized, neither parent has automatic legal authority over medical or financial decisions.

Projected federal estate tax exposure of approximately $8M on a $50M projected estate at second death (after the $30M MFJ exemption under OBBBA), with no current mitigation strategies in place — no ILIT, no GRAT, no IDGT, no SLAT, no QPRT, no GST allocation, no annual exclusion gifting program.
Georgia PTET election has not been filed. At Marcus's K-1 level this is leaving approximately $73,000/year of federal tax savings on the table (post-OBBBA SALT cap math).

Effective combined federal+GA tax rate is approximately 41% — well above what is achievable with PTET, optimized W-2/K-1 mix, a cash-balance retirement layer, cost segregation on the Kennesaw facility, §469 grouping, and an R&D credit study (HIS's custom controls and process-piping engineering work is a strong R&D candidate).

Catherine is not currently on payroll. That blocks 401(k) deferral capacity, individual disability insurability, backdoor Roth mechanics for her, and the family-employed-children custodial Roth path — all addressable by adding her at a defensible $40K–$80K W-2.
Underpayment exposure: with W-2 of $480K and K-1 of ~$3.4M, withholding/estimates are not currently calibrated to the 110% prior-year safe harbor. Approximately $40K of avoidable §6654 penalty.
$2M personal umbrella against a $33M–$46M net-worth profile. Material exposure — this is the single most undersized liability layer on the personal balance sheet.
Auto liability at $250K/$250K is below the underlying-limit floor most umbrella carriers require above $10M. Sub-umbrella mismatch.

No life insurance funding the buy/sell with Derek. Marcus's existing $2M term is personally-owned (estate-includible), expires 2031, and is not held in trust. Catherine's $500K term is materially under-sized.

Group LTD only on Marcus (60% replacement, 24-month own-occ then any-occ) — inadequate for an HNW owner. No individual DI. No coverage on Catherine. No business overhead expense (BOE) policy. No key-person coverage on Derek or the controller.
Cyber at $1M is likely under-sized given the hyperscale data-center customer mix and the contractual cyber requirements those customers typically push down.

Customer concentration: top 3 = 47% of 2024 revenue, top single = 22% (hyperscale data-center). Buyers discount owner-dependent and concentration-heavy specialty contractors by 25–35%; addressable through a 24-month diversification program and longer-term contracts.

Derek's economic position has not been formalized in years. The 12% profits interest vested in 2022; there is no current shareholder agreement language, no defined equity-path going forward, no SERP, no stay bonus, and no key-person life on him.

No formal valuation, no Q-of-E, and M&A counsel/banker not yet engaged. Standard pre-transaction sequence is to engage these at T-12 to T-18 months — meaning if the realistic window is 3 years, the engagement clock starts soon.

Approximately 95%+ of household net worth is concentrated in HIS equity. Personal taxable brokerage is $1.8M self-directed at Schwab (S&P-500-equivalent fund); Catherine's Rollover IRA is $340K; Marcus's 401(k) is $920K. The personal portfolio should be the diversification, not another concentration.
Catherine's $340K Rollover IRA blocks backdoor Roth mechanics for her via the pro-rata rule until that balance is cleared (most cleanly by rolling it into a 401(k) once she is on payroll).
529 plans are not yet open for any of the three children. Olivia (19, UGA sophomore) has limited remaining horizon; James (16) and Sophie (12) have meaningful runway for a front-load deployment.

Marcus's mother Eleanor (78) is independent today but is expected to need assisted-living or in-home care within 5–10 years. LTC status is unverified; Eleanor's balance sheet has not been formally documented. This is an addressable gap, not a crisis.

No DAF, no foundation, no CRUT, no CLAT, no charitable vehicle of any kind today — despite both of you holding clear and specific philanthropic intent (Marcus: trade scholarships at North Cobb Tech; Catherine: children's hospital board, family-foundation-with-children-in-governance hard-rule preference). The pre-transaction window is the most efficient time to fund these vehicles.

recommendations_business.intro_paragraph: The business-side recommendations are organized in the order they should be executed. Sections 1 and 2 — entity restructure and cash management — are foundational; nearly everything else (tax, estate, risk) sits on top of them. Section 3 is the tax program, split into items to implement this year, items to evaluate within 12 months, and longer-term considerations. Section 4 is the estate program, which is where the largest dollar transfers live but which depends on Section 1 completing first. Section 5 is the risk program. Section 6 is benefits and key-employee retention, including Derek. Section 7 is the pre-transaction sequence — Q-of-E, banker, fractional CFO, management bench, customer concentration. Read in order, the sequencing logic should be visible.

sections.label: [CORE SECTION]
sections.intro_paragraph: How your businesses, real estate, and personal interests are titled matters for liability protection, taxation, and the eventual sale or transfer of the enterprise. Today, HIS holds the $4.2M Kennesaw facility inside the operating LLC, has no holding-company layer, has no voting/non-voting recapitalization, and operates under a 2009 operating agreement that has never been refreshed for Derek's vested 12%. Four structural changes need to happen in sequence over the next 90–180 days, and the sequence matters — direct professional fees aggregate to roughly $40K–$80K, and the option value created downstream (recap discount alone is approximately $7.4M of estate-includible value removed) is more than 100x the cost.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Separate the real estate first.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Form Holloway Properties, LLC (Georgia). Transfer the Kennesaw facility from HIS via a documented §721/§351 contribution-and-distribution structure that preserves basis. Put a written, market-rate triple-net lease in place between HIS (tenant) and Holloway Properties (landlord). Engage a third-party broker to opine on market rent before execution; the IRS will look at this in any audit. Direct cost approximately $15K–$30K. Truist consent on the LOC is required — we will coordinate.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Execute the F-reorganization to insert a holdco.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Form Holloway Holdings (Georgia), have Marcus and Derek contribute their HIS interests in exchange for Holdco units, file Form 2553 (Holdco S-election) and Form 8869 (QSub election for HIS). This creates the holding-company layer that every downstream estate, charitable, and transaction-structuring move depends on. Direct cost approximately $20K–$35K; the option value (charitable gifting of Holdco units, GRAT funding, IDGT sale, stock-vs-asset transaction flexibility) exceeds $8M.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Recapitalize Marcus's 88% into voting and non-voting units.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Inside Holdco, recapitalize into voting (~10%) and non-voting (~78%) units. The non-voting interest is what gets used in the GRAT and IDGT planning in Section 4 — it allows you to transfer economic value without giving up control. Properly structured with a qualified appraisal, this produces a defensible 25%–35% combined LOM/minority discount on the gifted interest; at a $35.2M midpoint business value this is approximately $7.4M of estate-includible value removed at the recap step alone. Derek's consent is required on the recap of his 12%.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Replace the 2009 HIS operating agreement.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: M&A counsel drafts a replacement operating agreement reflecting the holdco structure, the recap, transfer restrictions, Derek's 12% economics, and modern buy/sell provisions coordinated with the cross-purchase life insurance program in Section 5. Direct cost approximately $5K–$15K; avoided transaction friction at deal-time is $25K–$100K plus weeks on the diligence calendar.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Calendar an annual entity-document audit.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Once the new structure is in place, an annual review of operating, shareholder, buy/sell, and inter-entity agreements catches drift before it shows up in transaction diligence. Annual review cost approximately $1K–$3K; avoided transaction friction $10K–$50K+.

closer_paragraph.label: Why this sequence matters
closer_paragraph.body: If you do the GRAT or IDGT sale before the recapitalization, you give up the valuation discount and lose meaningful estate-tax savings. If you transfer the real estate after the F-reorg rather than before, the contribution-and-distribution mechanics get more complex. The order is: real estate separation first, F-reorg and holdco second, recap third, replacement operating agreement fourth — then the estate work in Section 4 and the cost-seg/§469 work in Section 3B. Done in the right order over 90–180 days, the tax cost of the restructuring is essentially zero, the direct professional fees aggregate to $40K–$80K, and the option value created is in the eight figures.

cross_references.display_text: the cost segregation and §469 grouping in Section 3B, which require Holloway Properties to exist first
the GRAT, IDGT, SLAT, and QPRT planning in Section 4, which all use the post-recap non-voting units
the cross-purchase buy/sell in Section 5, coordinated with the replacement operating agreement

sections.label: [CORE SECTION]
sections.intro_paragraph: HIS operating cash today sits in a single Truist account at the bank rate. We do not have the current balance disclosed in our discovery file, but for context: every $1M of idle business cash earning 0.1% rather than a 4.5% reserve yield is approximately $44,000 per year of foregone return — money you could use to fund the buy/sell premium in Section 5 or the SERP funding in Section 6, with no operational risk. The first action below is to size the actual idle balance with the controller; everything else flows from that.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Size the idle balance with the controller.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: PSA + the controller pull the trailing 12 months of Truist operating-account balances and AP/AR cycles to identify the genuine idle layer (the cash that is not needed for next-30-day payables, payroll, tax payments, or seasonal swings). This unblocks the yield calculation we cannot complete from the discovery file alone.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Open a tiered business cash structure.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Three layers: (1) Operating account — 30 days of payables, full liquidity. (2) Working reserve — 90 days of operating expenses, money market or short laddered T-bills, currently yielding ~4.5%. (3) Strategic reserve — funds earmarked for tax payments, profit sharing, and key initiatives, in a higher-yield treasury or municipal sweep. We will model the right balance across the three based on your billing cycle and seasonality. Treasuries are state-tax-exempt in Georgia, which adds approximately 5.19% of state-tax efficiency to the yield calculation.

closer_paragraph.label: What this means
closer_paragraph.body: Once the idle balance is sized, the recurring yield this generates is meaningful — and recurring matters. The cash management restructure is one of the few items in the plan that pays for itself within 90 days and continues paying every year thereafter. We expect the recovered yield to fund a meaningful share of the buy/sell and key-person life premium recommended in Section 5.

cross_references.display_text: the buy/sell and key-person life premiums in Section 5, which the recovered yield helps fund
the SERP funding in Section 6

sections.label: [CORE SECTION]
sections.intro_paragraph: Your effective combined federal+Georgia tax rate is approximately 41% — well above what is achievable with PTET, an optimized W-2/K-1 mix, a cash-balance retirement layer, cost segregation on the Kennesaw facility, §469 grouping, and an R&D credit study. The tax program is split into three tiers: items to implement this year (3A), items to evaluate within 12 months once the entity restructure completes (3B), and longer-term considerations including the §1202 QSBS analysis (3C). The 3A items alone are approximately $130K–$160K of recurring annual savings, plus the one-time $40K underpayment penalty avoidance.

subsections.heading: 3A. Implement This Year
subsections.intro: These six items move quickly, do not depend on the entity restructure, and produce recurring annual savings or one-time penalty avoidance.

bullets.bold_imperative: File the Georgia PTET election for 2026.
bullets.briefing: Estimated annual federal tax savings: approximately $73,000 based on Marcus's projected 2026 K-1 income at the post-OBBBA SALT cap with phase-out. CPA models with-vs-without, confirms entity cash flow capacity to fund PTET via quarterly distributions adjustment, files Form 600S election with the HIS S-Corp return, makes Form 602-ES quarterly payments, and verifies the credit on the Georgia Form 500 personal returns at year-end. Election must be re-made each tax year.

bullets.bold_imperative: Optimize Marcus's W-2 vs K-1 mix.
bullets.briefing: Reasonable-compensation analysis (RCReports or equivalent) for a specialty mechanical contractor CEO at $40M+ revenue typically supports reducing Marcus's W-2 from $480K toward $360K, balancing FICA savings against the QBI deduction cap. Net annual benefit approximately $5K–$15K. Documented in the HIS tax file for audit defense; payroll updated forward-only at Paychex on the next cycle boundary.

bullets.bold_imperative: Add Catherine to HIS payroll at $40K–$80K.
bullets.briefing: Net annual benefit approximately $15K–$25K from the business deduction at the household marginal rate, plus enabling 401(k) deferrals, individual DI insurability after 6–12 months of W-2 history, backdoor Roth mechanics, and the family-employed-children path. Catherine's role is documented with a written job description (marketing/admin), defensible salary set against industry benchmark, deliverables tracked quarterly. This unlocks several other items in the plan.

bullets.bold_imperative: Put James and Olivia on payroll for legitimate work.
bullets.briefing: Estimated annual parents' tax savings approximately $6,300 from $7,500 wages × 2 eligible children at ~42% combined marginal rate, plus opening custodial Roth IRAs at Schwab funded to earned income. The tax savings is real but secondary; the multi-decade compound on a custodial Roth funded at 16 is the primary value (see Section 11). Documentation discipline matters — job descriptions, time logs, deliverables, quarterly file.

bullets.bold_imperative: Implement the §280A(g) Augusta Rule program.
bullets.briefing: Approximately $7,584 of annual federal+GA savings from $18,000 of business rent (12 days × $1,500) paid by HIS to the homeowner and excluded under §280A(g). Three written quotes from local Atlanta event venues establish the defensible market rate; calendar quarterly board/strategy meetings at the East Cobb residence with real agendas, attendees, and minutes; rental invoices flow from homeowner to HIS. Track day count rigorously; never approach 14 days.

bullets.bold_imperative: Calibrate withholding/estimates to the 110% safe harbor.
bullets.briefing: Approximately $40,000 of avoidable §6654 underpayment penalty given $480K W-2 + $3.4M K-1. CPA produces a current-year projection reflecting PTET, cost-seg, cash-balance, and projected K-1; computes required withholding/estimates against the 110% prior-year safe harbor (AGI > $150K); Marcus submits an updated W-4 to Paychex; quarterly estimates locked on the April/June/September/January schedule with a mid-year (September) reconciliation.

subsections.heading: 3B. Evaluate Within 12 Months
subsections.intro: These items unlock once the entity restructure (Section 1) completes — specifically, once the Kennesaw facility is inside Holloway Properties, LLC. They produce the largest single-year tax wins in the plan.

bullets.bold_imperative: Commission a cost segregation study on the Kennesaw facility.
bullets.briefing: Estimated first-year federal+GA tax savings approximately $531,594 — based on the $4.2M building basis, 25%–35% reclassification into 5- and 15-year property, OBBBA permanent 100% bonus depreciation, and combined marginal rate. Range $360K–$620K depending on the engineering study's reclassification percentage. PSA confirms the Kennesaw facility has been moved to Holloway Properties first (this is the gating prerequisite from Section 1), obtains feasibility estimates from 2–3 cost-seg firms, then commissions the full engineering-based study. CPA implements via Form 3115 (catch-up) or Form 4562 (current year) and establishes recapture tracking for §1245/§1250 at future disposition.
bullets.partner_role: Specialty Tax Credits firm + CPA + PSA

bullets.bold_imperative: File the §469(c)(7) grouping election alongside the cost-seg.
bullets.briefing: Without the grouping election, the cost-seg-driven accelerated depreciation is trapped as passive losses against passive-only income — and Marcus has very little passive income. With the grouping election (filed in the same return year as the cost-seg study), the depreciation offsets Marcus's active K-1 income. That is the unlock. CPA confirms common ownership and material participation in both Holloway Properties and HIS, files the §469(c)(7) election, and documents material participation going forward via time logs and decision-making evidence. The election is binding.

bullets.bold_imperative: Run the R&D credit feasibility on HIS's engineering work.
bullets.briefing: Estimated annual federal R&D credit approximately $40K–$120K (net of specialty firm 25% contingent fee) given $42.8M revenue and HIS's custom controls and process-piping engineering work. PSA engages a specialty R&D credit firm for a no-cost feasibility review; if positive, the firm runs the full study (engineering interviews, QRE quantification); CPA files Form 6765 federally and the Georgia R&D credit form. Renew annually as qualifying activities continue. Contemporaneous documentation is the audit-defense backbone.
bullets.partner_role: Specialty Tax Credits firm + CPA + PSA

bullets.bold_imperative: Coordinate multi-state PTET and apportionment.
bullets.briefing: HIS operates across GA, TN, AL, the Carolinas, and Northern FL. Per-state PTET regimes, apportionment, and nexus must be coordinated to avoid double-counting and capture state-specific optimizations. This is a low-priority module given GA-only residence — it is not a value-driver — but it gets calendared with the CPA so it does not surprise us in a transaction.

subsections.heading: 3C. Long-Term Considerations
subsections.intro: These items require deliberate decisions over a longer horizon and interact with the transaction structure itself.

bullets.bold_imperative: Run the §1202 QSBS eligibility analysis.
bullets.briefing: Approximately $3.57M–$7.14M (and as much as $10.7M with multiplication via SLAT/children's trusts) of federal capital-gains tax savings on $15M–$30M of QSBS exclusion at 23.8% LTCG+NIIT, assuming a 5-year hold post-C-Corp conversion. HIS as a specialty mechanical contractor is non-SSTB, which is the threshold gate. The decision is between Variation A (C-Corp from start — too late), B (convert mid-stream — most realistic), or C (multiplication via gifting). This is a Decision Needed item — the holding-period clock and transaction window have to align, and the C-Corp conversion has its own tax cost. PSA + M&A counsel + CPA will model and recommend before any commitment.
bullets.partner_role: M&A Counsel + CPA + Estate Attorney + PSA

closer_paragraph.label: Quantified impact
closer_paragraph.body: The 3A items alone produce approximately $130K–$160K of recurring annual federal+state savings, plus the one-time $40K underpayment-penalty avoidance. The 3B items add approximately $530K of first-year tax savings (cost-seg + §469 grouping, which work together) plus $40K–$120K/year of recurring R&D credit. The 3C §1202 analysis, if it converts, is a $3.6M–$10.7M one-time capital-gains saving at transaction. Aggregate first-year tax impact is in the range of $700K–$1.2M, with the §1202 upside additional and contingent on entity-conversion timing.

cross_references.display_text: the entity restructure in Section 1, which must complete before the cost-seg in 3B
the §1202 conversion-path decision (see Decisions Needed)
the cash-balance plan layer in Section 6, which interacts with the W-2/K-1 mix

sections.label: [CORE SECTION]
sections.intro_paragraph: Your wills date from 2014, when net worth was approximately $3M. They do not name a guardian for Sophie. There is no revocable trust, no ILIT, no DPOA, no healthcare directive, no HIPAA authorization for Olivia (now 19), no beneficiary review since 2014, and no transfer planning of any kind in place against a projected estate of approximately $50M at second death — which after the $30M MFJ exemption (2026 OBBBA) leaves approximately $20M taxable × 40% = $8M of federal estate tax due 9 months after the second death. Georgia has no estate tax. The estate program below is layered: foundation first (revocable trust, core documents, beneficiary audit, annual exclusion gifting, ILIT), receiving architecture second (children's trusts), transfer engines third (GRAT, IDGT sale, SLAT, QPRT, GST/dynasty), and specialty overlays fourth (NING/DING, CRUT/ILIT bailout, CLAT). Done in sequence, the cumulative estate-tax savings exceed the projected estate-tax exposure many times over.

subsections.heading: 4A. Foundation (0–60 days)
subsections.intro: These items are non-negotiable and execute first. They are not the headline transfers — they are the legal infrastructure the rest of the section depends on.

bullets.bold_imperative: Stand up a joint revocable living trust.
bullets.briefing: Approximately $100K of probate cost avoided on the ~$5M of probate-eligible assets (range $50K–$150K at GA 1%–3% attorney fees). Estate attorney drafts; both of you fund it by retitling the East Cobb residence, the Blue Ridge property, the Schwab taxable brokerage, and the post-recap voting Holdco interest. Annual review of trust assets and successor trustee designations.

bullets.bold_imperative: Replace the 2014 core document set.
bullets.briefing: Pour-over wills, financial POAs, healthcare directives, HIPAA authorizations, and guardianship designations for both of you (the 2014 wills do not name a guardian for Sophie — this is the most urgent single document in the plan). Direct cost approximately $8K–$18K. Separately, draft a HIPAA authorization and healthcare/financial POA for Olivia (age 19, legally an adult). Decision Needed: guardianship choice for Sophie.

bullets.bold_imperative: Run the beneficiary designation audit.
bullets.briefing: Beneficiary designations override wills. They are the single most-overlooked failure point in HNW estate planning. PSA pulls the current designations on Marcus's 401(k), Catherine's Rollover IRA, the Schwab brokerage TOD, and both life insurance policies, reconciles them against the new estate plan, executes updates with the estate attorney, and calendars an annual reconciliation review.

bullets.bold_imperative: Begin systematic annual exclusion gifting in January.
bullets.briefing: Approximately $1.216M of estate-tax savings over 20 years — principal-only, before compound growth. The full range with growth is $1.216M (principal-only at 40%) to $2.64M (with 7% compound). $19K × 2 spouses × 4 donees (Olivia, James, Sophie, Eleanor) × 20 years = $3.04M removed from the estate. PSA calendars and executes January gifts each year; CPA files Form 709 to report gift-splitting.

bullets.bold_imperative: Establish the ILIT and fund estate-liquidity insurance.
bullets.briefing: Approximately $3.6M of federal estate tax avoided on $9M of life insurance held in the ILIT (rather than personal-owned and pulled into the estate at §2042) at the 40% rate. ILIT applies for and owns the policies from inception to avoid the §2035 3-year lookback. Recommended structure: $10M survivorship UL on Marcus + Catherine (both insurable at preferred rates today) at approximately $80K–$120K of annual premium, sized to the projected ~$8M federal estate tax 9 months after second death. Crummey notice procedures established and documented annually.
bullets.partner_role: Estate Attorney + Insurance Broker + CPA + PSA

subsections.heading: 4B. Receiving Architecture (60–120 days)
subsections.intro: Before we transfer meaningful value out of the estate, the trusts that receive it have to exist. Children's trusts are that architecture.

bullets.bold_imperative: Create the children's trusts.
bullets.briefing: Approximately $4M–$8M of cumulative federal estate-tax savings over a 20-year program — these trusts are the receiving architecture for the annual exclusion gifts, the GRAT remainder, the IDGT distributions and growth, and (post-liquidity) charitable carve-outs. Decision Needed: per-child trusts versus single pot trust (firm-policy choice; we recommend per-child for clean accounting and the age-35 hard rule). Decision Needed: trustee selection — we recommend a corporate trustee. Estate attorney drafts; CPA obtains EINs and opens accounts; Crummey notice procedures established.

bullets.bold_imperative: Decide trust situs for the long-duration trusts.
bullets.briefing: Siting the dynasty/IDGT/GST trusts in Delaware or South Dakota (rather than Georgia, which is RAP-limited and imposes 5.19% state tax on trust income) compounds meaningfully over a 50-year horizon. Decision Needed: GA vs DE vs SD vs NV. Cost is modest; the deferred state-tax compounding is in the mid-seven to low-eight figures over horizon. Estate attorney (with co-counsel in the chosen state) drafts; corporate trustee in the chosen state for residency proof.

subsections.heading: 4C. Transfer Engines (4–24 months)
subsections.intro: These are the structures that move value out of your estate. They depend on Section 1 (recap, qualified appraisal of non-voting units) being complete.

bullets.bold_imperative: Fund a 3-Year Zeroed-Out GRAT with non-voting Holdco units.
bullets.briefing: Estimated value transferred to children's trusts free of gift tax over the 3-year term: approximately $4.2M, computed as $8.2M after-discount funding × ((1.18)^3 − (1.05)^3) at the §7520 hurdle of 5.0% (current at funding, May 2026) and 18% assumed annual business growth. Range is wide — $0 if the business stalls below the §7520 hurdle (the GRAT simply unwinds at zero gift cost), $6M+ if a transaction event during or just after the term unwinds the discount. Asymmetric in your favor.
bullets.partner_role: Estate Attorney + Valuation Provider + CPA + PSA

bullets.bold_imperative: Execute the sale to IDGT.
bullets.briefing: Approximately $6M–$10M (potentially up to $15M with favorable growth + transaction timing) of additional wealth transferred outside Marcus's estate via 9-year IDGT installment sale of post-recap non-voting units at AFR. The grantor-trust income tax burn — Marcus pays the trust's income tax annually as a backdoor tax-free gift to beneficiaries — is the secondary engine. Seed gift of ~10% of intended sale value funded first; §675(4) substitute power triggers grantor-trust status; CPA tax memo confirms.

bullets.bold_imperative: Fund a Spousal Lifetime Access Trust (SLAT).
bullets.briefing: Approximately $10.8M of federal estate tax avoided over a 25-year horizon by funding a $5M SLAT (typically with appreciating asset — securities or post-recap units) growing at 7%/year to ~$27M outside both spouses' estates, taxed at 40%. HEMS distributions to beneficiary-spouse + children, independent trustee. Decision Needed: funding asset selection.

bullets.bold_imperative: Evaluate a 12-year QPRT on the Blue Ridge vacation home.
bullets.briefing: Approximately $840K of federal estate tax avoided by transferring the $1.4M Blue Ridge home to a 12-year QPRT at the current §7520 of 5.0%, with appreciation passing outside the estate. Range $0–$1.5M ($0 floor if Marcus dies during the 12-year term, in which case the residence is fully back in the estate — this is the QPRT's binary risk). Decision Needed: confirm Catherine's long-term commitment to Blue Ridge (post-term, you would pay FMV rent to the trusts, which itself becomes additional outside-estate transfer).

bullets.bold_imperative: Allocate GST exemption to the dynasty trusts.
bullets.briefing: At rec-file projection scale (allocating $30M of GST exemption to dynasty trusts growing at 5%/year over 60 years), this is approximately $224M of GST tax avoided across two generations — a number that depends entirely on horizon, growth rate, and funding magnitude. Decision Needed (overlaps with situs above): GA vs DE/SD/NV for perpetual dynasty mechanics. CPA files Form 709 with explicit GST exemption allocation at every funding event; PSA maintains GST inclusion ratio tracking across all funding events.

subsections.heading: 4D. Specialty Overlays (12–24 months, transaction-coordinated)
subsections.intro: These specialty structures interact with the transaction itself and Catherine's and Marcus's charitable intent. They are not foundational; they are amplifiers.

bullets.bold_imperative: Pre-LOI charitable gift of non-voting Holdco units.
bullets.briefing: Approximately $900K–$2.4M of combined federal income tax + capital gains avoided on a pre-LOI gift of 5% of non-voting Holdco units to a DAF (or CRT/foundation), depending on final HIS valuation. Must occur before any binding letter of intent — once an LOI is signed, the IRS treats the gift as effectively a sale by you and disallows the structure. Qualified appraiser supports FMV with discounts; Form 8283 filed with the gift documentation.
bullets.partner_role: Valuation Provider + Estate Attorney + CPA + PSA

bullets.bold_imperative: Fund a FLIP-CRUT pre-transaction.
bullets.briefing: Approximately $1.1M of federal charitable deduction at funding for a $5M FLIP-CRUT, 5% unitrust, 20-year+ horizon, computed at the current §7520 of 5.0% (May 2026). FLIP variant fits illiquid Holdco-unit funding. Coordinate the appraisal with the pre-LOI gift above.

bullets.bold_imperative: Pair the CRUT with a wealth-replacement ILIT (CRUT/ILIT bailout).
bullets.briefing: Indicative range $2M–$8M of survivorship UL face owned by the wealth-replacement ILIT (midpoint $5M), so the family is not disinherited by the charitable gift. The CRUT generates lifetime income, charitable deduction, and a charity remainder; the ILIT replaces what charity receives with an income-tax-free death benefit to the family. Sized after the CRUT funding asset is selected and survivorship UL underwriting offers are received.
bullets.partner_role: Estate Attorney + Insurance Broker + CPA + PSA

bullets.bold_imperative: Evaluate NING/DING for the GA state-tax avoidance at sale.
bullets.briefing: Approximately $1.5M–$2.2M of GA 5.19% state tax avoidable on Marcus's share of HIS sale capital gain via a NING (NV) or DING (DE) trust funded pre-binding-sale. Range based on $32M–$48M valuation × 88% × estimated near-zero founder basis. Borderline at the GA rate — admin cost $25K–$50K is meaningful relative to benefit, and the structure has scrutiny risk if not executed with discipline. Decision Needed: proceed or not, post entity-restructure.

bullets.bold_imperative: Evaluate a 20-year zeroed-out grantor CLAT.
bullets.briefing: Family remainder gift-tax-free at end of a 20-year zeroed-out grantor CLAT — range $0 (if asset matches the §7520 hurdle of 5.0%) to ~$5M+ (if asset earns 8%) to ~$10M (at 10%). Highly sensitive to §7520 environment and asset performance; pairs cleanly with the pre-transaction Holdco gift if charitable intent is large. Specialist counsel evaluates CLAT vs GRAT/IDGT alternatives before commitment.

closer_paragraph.label: Combined estate impact
closer_paragraph.body: Stacked together — annual exclusion gifting (~$1.2M), ILIT estate-tax avoidance (~$3.6M), children's trusts cumulative (~$4M–$8M), GRAT remainder (~$4.2M midpoint), IDGT sale (~$6M–$10M), SLAT 25-year (~$10.8M), QPRT (~$840K) — the Section 4 program transfers approximately $30M–$45M of value outside the taxable estate, against a projected ~$8M federal estate tax exposure today and a much larger one if HIS continues to grow. The pre-LOI charitable gift, the FLIP-CRUT, and the wealth-replacement ILIT add another $3M–$8M of family + charitable benefit if charitable intent is funded at the indicative scale. Sequencing is the entire game: foundation first, receiving architecture second, transfer engines third, specialty overlays fourth — and every one of the transfer engines requires the recap from Section 1 to have closed.

cross_references.display_text: the holdco recapitalization in Section 1, which produces the non-voting units used here
the survivorship UL underwriting in Section 5, which this section depends on
the per-child vs pot trust, trust situs, and SLAT funding-asset decisions (see Decisions Needed)
the personal-side estate documents and beneficiary execution in Section 12

sections.label: [CORE SECTION]
sections.intro_paragraph: Insurance is one of the few financial decisions where the right answer is shaped by exposure, not opinion. Our approach is to size protection to specific dollar exposures, not generic rules of thumb. On the business side, four exposures are presently uncovered or under-covered: the buy/sell with Derek, Marcus's individual disability, business overhead expense, and key-person life on Derek and the controller. Cyber is materially under-sized given the customer mix. Personal-side risk (umbrella, LTC, Catherine's D&O) is addressed in Section 9.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Fund the cross-purchase buy/sell with Derek.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Aggregate annual term-life premium approximately $8K–$30K for two cross-purchase policies — Marcus owning ~$5M on Derek (cleanly funded personally) and Derek owning ~$5M starting layer on Marcus (funded at the entity level via bonus-and-loan, AFR-compliant, documented). 20-year convertible term, age 50–52 preferred non-smoker rates. Coordinate buy/sell terms with the replacement HIS operating agreement from Section 1; confirm trigger events, valuation methodology, and payment terms with M&A counsel.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Underwrite max-issue individual DI on Marcus.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Approximately $25,000/year (range $15K–$30K) for max-issue individual DI, own-occ to age 65/67, $25K–$35K/month benefit. Group LTD at 60% replacement with own-occ→any-occ at 24 months is inadequate for an HNW owner. Premium paid personally (post-tax) so benefits are tax-free under §104(a)(3). Coordinated with the buy/sell disability trigger.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Issue a Business Overhead Expense (BOE) policy.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Approximately $6,000/year (range $3K–$8K) at 1–2% of covered overhead. HIS as policyowner and beneficiary; Marcus as insured; 24-month benefit period. Premium paid by HIS as deductible §162 business expense. Material given $4M revolver, 87 FTE, $48M backlog — a 90+ day owner disability without BOE is the kind of event that breaks an enterprise's continuity narrative in transaction diligence.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Issue key-person life on Derek and the controller.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Aggregate approximately $6,000/year (range $3K–$10K) for two term policies at ~$2.5M face each. HIS is policyowner and beneficiary. Note: Derek is also covered under the cross-purchase buy/sell — the key-person layer covers a different economic loss (revenue/transition gap, not equity buyout). §101(j) notice-and-consent must be executed BEFORE policy issuance — non-negotiable. CPA files Form 8925 annually for employer-owned life insurance reporting.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Upgrade cyber liability to $3M–$5M.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Current $1M is materially under-sized given the hyperscale data-center customer mix and the contractual cyber requirements those customers typically push down. Quote at $3M, $4M, and $5M tiers; verify ransomware, social engineering, and business interruption inclusions. Coordinate with HIS IT for incident response plan documentation aligned with the new policy. Annual renewal review.

closer_paragraph.label: What this means
closer_paragraph.body: Total business-side new annual premium is in the range of approximately $45K–$75K (DI + BOE + buy/sell + key-person + cyber upgrade), against multiple individual exposures each worth $1M–$5M+ to the enterprise on the wrong day. The buy/sell premium and key-person premium are partially funded by the recovered yield from Section 2's cash restructure. Underwriting is time-sensitive: both Marcus and Catherine are insurable at preferred rates today, and the §101(j) notice-and-consent on Derek and the controller must be in writing before any policy issues.

cross_references.display_text: the recovered yield in Section 2 that helps fund these premiums
the replacement operating agreement in Section 1, where the buy/sell terms are codified
the personal-side risk program in Section 9 (umbrella, LTC, Catherine's D&O)

sections.label: [CORE SECTION]
sections.intro_paragraph: Two related programs live in this section: (1) the retirement plan stack — 401(k), profit-sharing, possible cash-balance overlay, mega-backdoor and backdoor Roth, plan amendment for SECURE 2.0 — and (2) the key-employee retention architecture for Derek, the controller, and the broader bench going into a transaction. The retirement stack produces approximately $26K–$110K of recurring annual tax savings depending on cash-balance design. The retention architecture (SERP, stay bonus, restricted §162 bonus, Derek equity-path) is the difference between a buyer paying for a durable enterprise and a buyer discounting for owner dependence.

subsections.heading: 6A. Retirement Plan Stack
subsections.intro: We optimize what's there before adding new layers.

bullets.bold_imperative: Maximize Marcus's 401(k) deferral with Roth catch-up.
bullets.briefing: Approximately $12,025 of current-year federal tax savings on Marcus's $32,500 deferral (deferral + catch-up) at ~37% marginal — household range expands to $18K–$25K once Catherine is on payroll and enrolled. PSA confirms the Empower plan supports Roth deferrals/catch-up; if not, the plan amendment below is the path. Marcus submits the deferral election; Catherine enrolls after the spousal payroll change in Section 3A.

bullets.bold_imperative: Amend the plan document for Roth/after-tax mechanics.
bullets.briefing: Plan amendment is a low-cost compliance enabler that unlocks SECURE 2.0 Roth catch-up compliance and mega-backdoor Roth capacity downstream. Standalone dollar value flows through dependent recommendations, not a standalone number.

bullets.bold_imperative: Restate the plan for SECURE Act and SECURE 2.0.
bullets.briefing: Operational compliance item — preserves plan qualification by incorporating statutory changes into the plan document. No standalone economic value to quantify; required.

bullets.bold_imperative: Confirm SECURE 2.0 Roth catch-up compliance for 2026.
bullets.briefing: Compliance mandate — ensures Marcus's $8,000 catch-up is preserved as Roth treatment for 2026, avoiding loss of catch-up capacity if the plan does not support Roth.

bullets.bold_imperative: Add a cross-tested profit-sharing layer.
bullets.briefing: Approximately $14,615 of recurring annual current-year tax savings (Marcus profit-sharing layer of $39,500 filling the §415(c) gap to $72,000 × 37% marginal). Tax-deferred growth additional. TPA amends the plan document; CPA funds by the entity tax-filing deadline; annual cross-testing nondiscrimination analysis.

bullets.bold_imperative: Evaluate a cash-balance plan overlay.
bullets.briefing: Range of net annual economic benefit $0–$110K (midpoint $60K) from a cash-balance plan layered on the existing 401(k). Range is wide because the workforce carry-along across 87 FTE under cross-tested design can erode owner-favorable benefit toward zero. Decision Needed: proceed or not, given the 3–5 year transaction window vs the ~3–5 year minimum durability the plan needs to defend on audit. Actuarial firm + DB-specialist TPA design analysis first; client decision second.
bullets.partner_role: DB-specialist TPA + Actuary + ERISA Counsel + CPA

bullets.bold_imperative: Open mega-backdoor Roth capacity for Marcus.
bullets.briefing: Approximately $25,000/year of incremental Roth contribution capacity (range $0–$39,500 depending on profit-sharing fill). Tax-free growth on this balance compounds for life; immediate tax cost is near zero. Triggered after the plan amendment supports after-tax + in-plan conversion.

bullets.bold_imperative: Run the backdoor Roth IRA for Marcus.
bullets.briefing: Approximately $8,600 of annual non-deductible traditional IRA contribution + immediate Roth conversion (50+). Marcus has no pre-tax IRA balance to contaminate the pro-rata rule. Catherine is blocked by her $340K Rollover IRA pro-rata until that balance is rolled into a 401(k) — which becomes feasible once she is on payroll per Section 3A.

bullets.bold_imperative: Pre-transaction termination of the cash-balance plan (if established).
bullets.briefing: Pre-transaction termination of any cash-balance plan keeps buyer diligence clean and avoids reversion-tax exposure (up to 50% of any over-funded excess on inadvertent reversion). Sequencing tied to LOI / target close date once that timing is set. This is a future item, contingent on whether the cash-balance plan is established at all.

subsections.heading: 6B. Key-Employee Retention & Derek's Equity Path
subsections.intro: The retention architecture is what a buyer actually pays for in a specialty contractor — the bench that runs the business after Marcus steps back.

bullets.bold_imperative: Stand up a §409A-compliant SERP for Derek and the controller.
bullets.briefing: Worked-example COLI premium approximately $25,000/year per primary key employee at Holloway-style sizing ($80K/year SERP for Derek, $40K/year for the controller). 7-year cliff vest tied to transaction event or 7 years of continued service, whichever first. §101(j) notice-and-consent signed BEFORE COLI policy issuance. Annual administration includes vesting tracking, balance reporting, Form 8925.

bullets.bold_imperative: Build a transaction stay-bonus pool of 1–3% of expected transaction value.
bullets.briefing: Approximately $400K–$1.2M pool sized to the $32M–$48M preliminary transaction range (1–3% × $40M midpoint = $400K–$1.2M). Recipient list includes Derek, the controller, lead estimators, lead PMs. Per-recipient agreements drafted by M&A counsel: closing trigger, vesting, separation provisions, non-compete coordination. Communicate on banker-recommended timing (typically post-LOI). Closing + 12-month-anniversary payouts.

bullets.bold_imperative: Layer in a restricted §162 bonus for the next tier of leaders.
bullets.briefing: §162 simplicity with retention pull via restrictive endorsement. Sizing depends on selected key-employee comp targets and vesting schedule, not yet specified for HIS. Decision Needed: recipient list and target amounts.

bullets.bold_imperative: Formalize Derek's equity path.
bullets.briefing: Derek's 12% vested in 2022 and has not been formalized since. The replacement operating agreement from Section 1 captures current economics; the equity path forward — whether to move toward broader profits-interest grants, deferred grants tied to transaction, or a buyout-on-event mechanic — is a separate decision. Decision Needed: target ownership % and mechanism. CPA models tax treatment of each variant.

bullets.bold_imperative: Evaluate profits-interest grants for the broader bench (gated on entity).
bullets.briefing: Profits-interest grants would be tax-efficient under Rev. Proc. 93-27 BUT require restructuring HIS away from S-Corp taxation first, or routing the grants through a separate LLC layer. Computation gated on the entity restructure outcome from Section 1 and final grant percentages. Decision Needed: separate grant LLC layer vs full restructure path.

closer_paragraph.label: Why this sequence matters
closer_paragraph.body: The retirement stack and the key-employee retention architecture answer different questions, but they show up in the same financial conversation: how much of the enterprise's value is durable without Marcus. The retirement stack pulls cash off the operating company's tax bill annually; the retention architecture protects the people who run the business when Marcus steps back. If the cash-balance plan is established, the pre-transaction termination sequence matters — buyer diligence on an open cash-balance plan with reversion exposure is a recurring source of friction, and we plan around it from the start.

cross_references.display_text: the W-2/K-1 mix and Catherine's payroll in Section 3A, which interact with deferral capacity
the buy/sell on Derek in Section 5, distinct from the SERP and stay bonus here
the pre-transaction sequence in Section 7, where stay bonuses and bench-building activate
the cash-balance, profits-interest, and Derek-equity-path decisions (see Decisions Needed)

sections.label: [OPTIONAL — included because of pre-transaction posture]
sections.intro_paragraph: You are not actively in market today — but two unsolicited approaches in 14 months means a 3–5 year window is realistic and may be shorter. Buyers pay for the same things in this vertical: clean books, durable management, reduced concentration, and proven Q-of-E. Owner-dependent, concentration-heavy specialty contractors discount 25–40%. The recommendations below are the sequence that closes those gaps over the next 18–24 months — and they are the highest-dollar items in this entire plan, by a wide margin.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Engage a fractional CFO at T-18 to T-12 months.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Approximately $80K–$200K/year in cost. Scope: monthly close, board-grade reporting, working capital optimization, transaction prep. Industrial-services / specialty contractor M&A experience required. ROI flows through the Q-of-E and banker outcomes below — direct attribution is conservative, but the cleaner reporting is what buyers actually price against.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Close management bench gaps over the next 12–18 months.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Approximately $6M–$12M of preserved transaction value (range from 20–40% owner-dependent business discount applied to a $30M baseline). Org-chart analysis identifies critical function gaps (COO/operations, CFO/finance, sales leader, key technical lead). Decision Needed per gap: hire externally, promote internally, or fractional contract. Compensation packages coordinated with the stay bonus, SERP, and any phantom equity from Section 6.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Run a 24-month customer concentration program.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Approximately $5M–$7M of preserved transaction value. HIS top single customer is 22% (hyperscale data center); top 3 = 47%. 25–35% discount risk on $30M baseline. The program: sales-team incentive realignment toward net-new accounts and broadening within non-concentrated accounts, customer expansion plans for non-concentrated accounts, and a 3-year-minimum long-term contract negotiated with the largest concentration customer. Quarterly KPI dashboard tracks concentration metrics.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Commission an independent Q-of-E at T-12 to T-18 months.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Approximately $1.5M–$4.5M of preserved transaction value (5–15% on a $30M baseline) net of $75K–$125K Q-of-E engagement cost. Independent firm — separate from existing CPA — for diligence credibility. Address the issues the report identifies (clean up books, normalize accounting, document customer concentration) in the months that follow. Refresh 6 months later if material time elapses before going to market.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Engage M&A banker at T-12 months.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Approximately $4.4M–$9.3M of net premium captured from running a competitive sale process — 15–30% premium on a $30M baseline less estimated 1–2% banker fee on the post-premium transaction. Shortlist 2–3 boutique LMM M&A finalists with industrial-services / specialty contractor specialty. Decision Needed: banker selection. M&A counsel negotiates the engagement letter (success-fee structure, minimums, expense reimbursement, carve-outs). CIM development with banker; full sell-side process from buyer outreach through LOIs through diligence and close.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Document a written succession & continuity plan.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Distinct from buy/sell financial mechanics. Covers interim leadership, decision authority, banking signatures (Truist), customer/vendor communication. Eliminates operational paralysis on owner death or disability. Decision Needed: interim leadership designation. Reviewed annually.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Maintain pre-transaction diversification discipline.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Approximately 60–85% of household net worth is currently in HIS equity. Resist the pre-transaction pattern of moving the personal portfolio aggressively into private markets or adjacent construction-sector exposure before a sale — the personal portfolio should be the diversification, not another concentration. Quarterly review of the household allocation against this rule.

closer_paragraph.label: Why the range is wide
closer_paragraph.body: The dollar ranges in this section are wide for a reason: the value-preservation impact of a fractional CFO, a clean Q-of-E, a competitive banker process, a closed management bench, and a diversified customer book all move in the same direction at the same time, and they overlap. We avoid double-counting by attributing them to distinct causal mechanisms — the bench closes the owner-dependence discount, the concentration program closes the concentration discount, the Q-of-E preserves what diligence would otherwise erode, and the banker captures premium from competition. Combined, the pre-transaction program preserves and captures multiple times the entirety of all professional fees described in Sections 1–6 of this plan.

cross_references.display_text: the stay-bonus pool and SERP in Section 6, which activate alongside this sequence
the pre-LOI charitable gift window in Section 4D, which closes on LOI signing
the Advisory Team page for current partner gaps (M&A counsel, banker, valuation/Q-of-E, fractional CFO)

recommendations_personal.intro_paragraph: The business is the first place we invest. The personal balance sheet is the second — it is what gives you flexibility, independence, and a soft landing if anything happens to HIS or to either of you. Today, roughly 85-90% of household net worth sits inside the operating company. The work in this lens is about building the diversification, protection, and structural readiness around that concentration so that your personal life does not depend on the business going perfectly. Five sections follow: personal cash and investment, personal risk management, personal estate planning, family and education, and charitable strategy.

sections.label: [PERSONAL — for owner(s)]
sections.intro_paragraph: You have approximately $310,000 of personal cash sitting in a Truist checking account at near-zero yield, and a $1.8M self-directed taxable brokerage at Schwab that is invested in plain index funds with no tax-management overlay. Neither is wrong — both are leaving money on the table. With a 3-5 year transaction window ahead and a 95%+ concentration ratio in HIS today, the personal portfolio's job over the next few years is twofold: deliberately diversify away from the business, and deliver every basis point of after-tax return the structure can produce. The work below is layered cash management, a tax-aware equity overlay, asset location across taxable and retirement accounts, and a discipline rule for what NOT to do during the pre-transaction window.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Layer the $310K of personal cash into operating, emergency, and near-term tiers.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Restructure into three layers: 1-2 months of household spend in a Truist operating account; 6-12 months of expenses in a high-yield savings or money-market fund at Schwab (currently ~4.5%); and 12-36 months of near-term spending needs in a short Treasury ladder (currently ~4.7%, GA-state-tax-exempt). Estimated incremental yield: approximately $18,000 per year on the current balance, with no liquidity sacrifice. We will set the layer sizes against your actual lifestyle outflow ($420K-$480K/year).

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Open a Treasury Direct or Schwab-brokerage Treasury allocation for the near-term layer.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: U.S. Treasury interest is exempt from Georgia state tax (5.19%); CDs and money-market funds are not. On a meaningful Treasury holding the state-tax pickup is real, and the credit and liquidity profile is unmatched. We will configure a laddered structure (3/6/12-month rungs) and auto-reinvest at maturity. CPA coordinates the Form 500 schedule adjustment at filing to capture the state exemption.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Replace the S&P-500-equivalent fund with a direct indexing SMA.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Direct indexing replicates an index using the underlying individual stocks — same return profile, but it systematically harvests tax losses on individual positions. On the $1.8M taxable balance, expected after-tax alpha is approximately 75 bps, or about $13,500 per year (range $9,000-$18,000). Particularly valuable in years with large business income — like every year you hold HIS, and especially the year of a transaction when realized gains spike. Migration handled gradually if existing positions carry meaningful embedded gains.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Implement asset location across taxable, 401(k), and Catherine's Rollover IRA.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Tax-inefficient assets (taxable bonds, REITs, high-turnover equity) belong in the 401(k) and IRA; tax-efficient assets (broad equity, munis, direct-indexed stocks) belong in taxable. On the combined $3.06M tax+retirement portfolio, expected after-tax improvement is approximately $23,000 per year (range $15,300-$30,600). Subject to Empower 401(k) menu constraints, which we will work around.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Add a Georgia-specific muni SMA for the taxable fixed-income sleeve.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: GA municipal bonds are exempt from both federal and GA state tax for Georgia residents. After-tax pickup vs. taxable corporates of comparable credit is roughly $4,300/year on a $720K fixed-income sleeve (range $2,000-$12,000 depending on sleeve size). Credit-quality discipline: minimum AA-/A1; avoid AMT private-activity issues at your income level.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Run quarterly tax-loss harvesting alongside direct indexing.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Even with direct indexing in place, we will run a discrete quarterly TLH review across all taxable accounts, with cross-account wash-sale awareness (Catherine's Rollover IRA can taint a sale on her side). The benefit largely overlaps with direct indexing — it is not additive — but the discipline matters in down quarters when the harvest opportunity is largest. Year-end summary delivered to the CPA for realized-gain offset and carryforward tracking.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Hold the line: no further concentration in HIS or construction-sector exposure.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Resist the pre-transaction pattern of moving the personal portfolio aggressively into private markets, single-stock concentration, or thematic bets adjacent to your business. Your concentration risk is already very high through HIS itself — an estimated 60-85% of household net worth depending on valuation. The personal portfolio should be the diversification, not another concentration. We will track this quarterly against an explicit IPS guardrail.

closer_paragraph.label: Quantified impact
closer_paragraph.body: Stacked together, the cash and investment changes produce roughly $59,000 per year of incremental after-tax return — approximately $18,000 from cash layering, $13,500 from direct indexing, $23,000 from asset location, and $4,300 from the muni sleeve, with TLH coordinated to avoid double-counting. That is meaningful in any year, but the real reason to do this work now is that a transaction event is going to generate the largest realized gain of your life, and the tax-management infrastructure needs to be running long before then to be useful at the moment it matters most.
