the personal estate planning in Section 10

sections.label: [PERSONAL — for owner(s)]
sections.intro_paragraph: Insurance is one of the few financial decisions where the right answer is shaped by exposure, not opinion. Today, your umbrella liability is $2M against a household net worth in the high $30M-to-mid-$40M range, your auto liability is $250K/$250K (which fails the underlying-coverage test for any meaningful umbrella), Marcus's group LTD is a thin layer with the wrong own-occupation definition for a HNW owner, and Catherine has no individual disability coverage at all. These are sizing problems, not philosophical ones. Each gap below is matched to a specific dollar exposure.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Raise the personal umbrella to $35M-$50M.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Recommended face amount range $35M-$50M, sized to current and post-transaction net worth ($33M-$46M). Layers above $10M typically require a specialty HNW carrier — Chubb, AIG Private Client, or PURE — which we will quote alongside the primary. We will quote at $25M, $35M, and $50M tiers so you can see the marginal cost of each step. This is the single largest unfunded liability on your personal balance sheet today.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Raise primary auto and home liability to umbrella-compliant limits first.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Before binding the umbrella, lift auto liability to at least $500K/$500K and home liability to $1M to satisfy the underlying-coverage requirement that all major umbrella carriers impose. Cost is modest. This is a gating step — without it, the umbrella claim can be denied or the policy non-renewed at the next cycle. We coordinate with your Allstate agent (or replace if a HNW carrier is moving the umbrella).

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Underwrite a max-issue individual DI policy on Marcus.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: The current group LTD has a 24-month own-occ definition that switches to any-occ — meaning after two years, if Marcus could theoretically work at a different job, benefits stop. For a HNW owner, that is the wrong product. Replace with an individual policy that is true own-occupation to age 65/67, $25K-$35K/month benefit, premium paid personally to keep benefits tax-free under §104(a)(3). Estimated premium $15K-$30K/year. MassMutual's max-issue program is the typical fit. Coordinate with the buy/sell disability trigger so the two policies stack rather than offset.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Plan Catherine's individual DI for after she has 6-12 months of W-2 history.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Catherine becomes meaningfully more difficult to insure with age and any health change. Once she is on payroll at HIS (see Section 3) and has built 6-12 months of W-2 history, we underwrite an individual DI policy on her. This is sequenced after the payroll change is made — the W-2 record is what enables the underwriting.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Evaluate hybrid LTC coverage on both lives.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: At Marcus 52 and Catherine 49, both healthy and insurable at preferred rates, this is the cheapest and most flexible window for LTC coverage. A hybrid life-and-LTC product (single-pay or 10-pay) at $100K-$250K per insured produces roughly $200K-$750K of death benefit and $400K-$1.5M of LTC benefit pool depending on carrier and structure. Three things need to lock before binding: your premium budget, the underwriting offers from at least two carriers, and the product structure choice (LTC pool separate from death benefit vs. acceleration of death benefit). We will model the comparison and bring it back for decision.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Add personal D&O for Catherine's nonprofit boards.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Catherine sits on two nonprofit boards (the children's hospital and one other). Verify each entity's D&O coverage is adequate, then add a personal D&O endorsement (typically $1K-$3K/year through a HNW carrier) to backstop entity coverage gaps. Standard nonprofit D&O often has carve-outs that leave the individual director exposed; a personal endorsement closes them.

closer_paragraph.label: What this means
closer_paragraph.body: Insurance fixes here are not about generating return — they are about not giving back what you have already built. The umbrella alone closes a roughly $40M+ exposure; the DI work protects $3M+ of annual income from a disability event the group plan would stop covering after 24 months; the LTC work prevents a multi-hundred-thousand-dollar drain on the retirement portfolio at the back end of life. Aggregate annual cost across all of this work is in the $35K-$60K range, depending on the LTC structure chosen — small relative to what is being protected.

cross_references.display_text: the buy/sell and key-person program in Section 5
the ILIT and survivorship UL in Section 10

sections.label: [PERSONAL — for owner(s)]
sections.intro_paragraph: Your wills date from 2014 — when household net worth was a small fraction of where it sits today, before Sophie was born, before HIS scaled to its current size. There is no revocable trust, no ILIT, no DPOA, no healthcare directive, no HIPAA authorization for Olivia (now age 19 and legally an adult), and no beneficiary review since the last update. Projected federal estate tax at second death, on a current-trajectory estate of approximately $50M, is roughly $8M against the $30M MFJ exemption — and that exposure grows as HIS appreciates. The work in this section divides into three layers: foundation documents (must happen first), wealth-transfer trusts that move value out of the estate, and the liquidity layer that pays the eventual tax bill without forcing a fire-sale of business interests.

subsections.heading: 10A. Foundation — must happen in the first 60 days
subsections.intro: These three items are non-negotiable starting points. Without them, the trusts and life insurance below either cannot fund or fund into the wrong structure.

bullets.bold_imperative: Replace the 2014 wills with a full document set including a joint revocable living trust.
bullets.briefing: Estate attorney drafts joint revocable living trust for Marcus and Catherine, plus pour-over wills, financial POAs, healthcare directives, HIPAA authorizations, and updated guardianship designations (the 2014 wills do not name a guardian for Sophie). Direct cost $8K-$18K. The revocable trust avoids approximately $100,000 of Georgia probate cost on the ~$5M of probate-eligible assets (excluding business equity), and it gives you a single clean home for retitling residences and brokerage accounts. Pending decision on guardianship — see Decisions Needed.

bullets.bold_imperative: Draft Olivia's separate adult document set.
bullets.briefing: Olivia turned 18 a year ago. Without her own HIPAA authorization and healthcare/financial POA, you legally cannot access her medical information or act on her behalf in an emergency — even though she is your daughter. Cost is small; the gap is acute. Estate attorney drafts as part of the family document set.

bullets.bold_imperative: Audit and re-execute every beneficiary designation.
bullets.briefing: Beneficiary designations on retirement accounts and life insurance override your will. They are the single most-overlooked failure point in HNW estate planning, and yours have not been touched since before the trust structure existed. We will inventory every designation across Marcus's 401(k), Catherine's Rollover IRA, the existing term life policies, and any annuity or TOD/POD account, then update each one to align with the new revocable trust and (where appropriate) the ILIT. Annual review thereafter.

subsections.heading: 10B. Wealth-transfer trusts — moving value out of the estate
subsections.intro: These structures sit on top of the foundation and move economic value out of your taxable estate. They are sequenced after the entity recap (Section 1) so that the non-voting interests with valuation discounts are available to fund them.

bullets.bold_imperative: Start the annual exclusion gifting program in January.
bullets.briefing: $19,000 per donee × 2 spouses × 4 donees (Olivia, James, Sophie, Eleanor) = $152,000 of annual exclusion capacity per year. Over 20 years that removes $3.04M from the estate before growth, producing approximately $1.2M of estate-tax savings on principal alone — and roughly $1.9M-$2.6M when you factor in compound growth on the gifted assets. Start this year; it does not require any other structure to be in place. CPA files Form 709 documenting gift-splitting. Pending decision: cash gifts to Olivia/Eleanor vs. Crummey-noticed contributions to children's trusts.

bullets.bold_imperative: Create the children's trusts (per-child or single pot).
bullets.briefing: These are the receiving vehicles for the annual gifts, the GRAT remainder (Section 4), and the IDGT distributions. Structured with distributions tightly controlled before age 35 — directly reflecting your hard rule about no unrestricted access to inherited wealth before then. Estate-tax savings over a 20-year transfer architecture: approximately $4M-$8M cumulative. Pending decisions: per-child trusts vs. single pot trust, and corporate vs. individual trustee — both surfaced in Decisions Needed.

bullets.bold_imperative: Fund a SLAT with $5M of appreciating assets.
bullets.briefing: A Spousal Lifetime Access Trust uses a portion of the lifetime exemption to move appreciating assets out of both spouses' estates while preserving indirect access through HEMS distributions to the beneficiary-spouse. On a $5M funding growing at 7%/year over 25 years (to roughly $27M outside the estate), federal estate tax avoided is approximately $10.8M (range $4M-$18M depending on growth). Pending decision: which appreciating asset to fund — non-voting Holdco units, Blue Ridge, or marketable securities. Funded after the entity recap so non-voting units with valuation discounts are an option.

bullets.bold_imperative: Move the Blue Ridge vacation home into a 12-year QPRT.
bullets.briefing: Transfers the $1.4M Blue Ridge home to a Qualified Personal Residence Trust at the current §7520 rate of 5.0% (May 2026), with appreciation passing outside the estate. Estimated estate tax avoided: approximately $840,000 ($2.5M projected future value − $400K gift value × 40%). At term end, you transition to a FMV rent lease back from the trust, which is itself an additional outside-estate transfer. Mortality risk: if Marcus dies during the 12-year term, the residence comes back into the estate (no benefit). This is the right candidate property because Blue Ridge has long-term family use and minimal financing complications. Pending confirmation of long-term use intent — see Decisions Needed.

bullets.bold_imperative: Allocate GST exemption to a dynasty trust at every funding event.
bullets.briefing: Every time you fund a long-duration trust (children's trusts, SLAT, IDGT), the CPA explicitly allocates GST exemption on Form 709. With $30M of allocated exemption sited in a perpetual-dynasty jurisdiction (Delaware or South Dakota — Georgia has a Rule Against Perpetuities limit), and 60 years of compounding at 5%, the structure shelters approximately $224M from generation-skipping tax across two generations. Pending decision on situs — surfaced in Decisions Needed.

subsections.heading: 10C. Liquidity layer — paying the eventual tax bill
subsections.intro: Even with aggressive transfer planning, there will be a federal estate tax due 9 months after the second death. Without dedicated liquidity, the estate is forced to liquidate assets — potentially including business interests at the wrong moment — to pay it.

bullets.bold_imperative: Form an ILIT and have it apply for the policies from inception.
bullets.briefing: Estate attorney drafts the Irrevocable Life Insurance Trust with a corporate or non-beneficiary individual trustee. The ILIT — not Marcus or Catherine — applies for, owns, and is the beneficiary of the policies from day one. This avoids the §2035 three-year lookback that would otherwise pull death benefit back into the estate. Crummey notice procedures and annual premium funding cycle established at formation.

bullets.bold_imperative: Issue $10M of survivorship UL inside the ILIT.
bullets.briefing: Survivorship UL pays at the second death — exactly when the federal estate tax becomes due. $10M face amount is sized to a projected $8M federal estate tax obligation (estate ~$50M, $30M MFJ exemption, $20M taxable × 40%) with cushion. Estimated annual premium $80K-$120K for both spouses at preferred rates given excellent health. Funded via Crummey-noticed gifts to the ILIT. Federal estate tax avoided on the $10M of proceeds (because the ILIT, not the estate, is the policy owner): approximately $3.6M.

closer_paragraph.label: Combined estate impact
closer_paragraph.body: Done in sequence — foundation documents in 60 days, transfer trusts after the entity recap, liquidity layer issued from the ILIT — the personal estate program produces roughly $20M-$30M+ of cumulative federal estate-tax savings across the program (annual exclusion gifting ~$1.2M-$2.6M, children's-trust architecture ~$4M-$8M, SLAT ~$10.8M, QPRT ~$840K, ILIT-owned survivorship UL ~$3.6M), plus a $224M GST shelter over a 60-year dynasty horizon for the next generation. The cost of doing it well — attorney fees, appraisals, annual administration, insurance premiums — is a small fraction of one year's savings. The cost of NOT doing it well is what gets paid to the IRS at the second death.

sections.label: [OPTIONAL — included because of three children at planning-relevant ages]
sections.intro_paragraph: Three children at three very different ages — Olivia 19 (UGA sophomore), James 16 (high school junior, eligible for legitimate family-business work), Sophie 12 (long horizon) — create very different planning windows. Layered on top is Catherine's deeply held view that philanthropy is how the children learn responsibility for wealth they did not create, and the family hard rule of no unrestricted access to inherited wealth before age 35. The work below addresses education funding, custodial Roth IRAs through the family-employed-children path, eventual support of Marcus's mother Eleanor, and the governance scaffolding that makes the trust architecture in Section 10 actually work the way you want it to as the children become adults.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Open Georgia Path2College 529s and front-load $190K each for James and Sophie.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Open one 529 per beneficiary with a parent owner. Year-1 deployment: $190K front-load each for James (16, ~6 years to college) and Sophie (12, ~6 years to college plus possible graduate horizon), gift-split between Marcus and Catherine using the 5-year-averaging election on Form 709. Olivia (sophomore) gets a smaller annual contribution rather than a front-load — the time horizon is too short. Year-1 deployment total ~$380K; projected value at college start ~$700K-$900K range based on a 13-year compound at 7%.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Track each 529's 15-year clock for the eventual Roth rollover.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: SECURE 2.0 lets unused 529 balances roll into the beneficiary's Roth IRA — up to $35,000 per beneficiary lifetime, subject to a 15-year account age and earned-income requirements. For three children, that is $105,000 of next-generation Roth seed capital, projected to compound tax-free over 30-50 years to roughly $800K-$2.5M. We calendar each account's 15-year milestone and execute when each child has earned income.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Put James and Olivia on payroll and fund custodial Roth IRAs.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Document each child's role with a real job description, hours, and deliverables (marketing/admin support, scanning, basic finance work — what they actually can do). Defensible wage rate compliant with GA child-labor minimum wage. Add to Paychex, issue W-2 at year-end. Then open a custodial Roth IRA at Schwab and fund up to lesser of earned income or the annual Roth limit ($7,500 in current figures). A single year's $7,500 contribution at age 16 compounds at 7% for 49 years to approximately $230,000 — tax-free. Do this every year you can. Pairs with the family-employed-children tax savings of approximately $6,300/year on the parents' return (Section 3).

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Have the discovery conversation with Eleanor.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Marcus's mother Eleanor (78, Marietta, currently independent and in good health) is on the path to needing assisted-living, in-home care, or both within 5-10 years. Industry-standard care-cost projections for that horizon range $300K-$1M+ depending on care intensity and duration. Before quantifying anything, we need her balance sheet, income, and confirmation of whether she has any LTC insurance (you believe she does not). PSA leads the conversation jointly with Marcus. Once we have the picture, we can model the family-contribution gap, evaluate whether LTC underwriting is still possible at her age, and put basic legal documentation in place.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Codify the Holloway family values into a written mission statement.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: The trade-scholarships theme (North Cobb Tech), Catherine's children's-hospital work, the dignity-of-skilled-trades view, and the stewardship-of-unearned-wealth principle all need to live somewhere durable. A written family mission statement gives trustees discretion language they can actually apply, and it gives the children a shared frame as they enter governance roles. We facilitate the values conversation; estate attorney integrates the language into trust and foundation documents.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Establish an annual family meeting cadence.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Once-a-year family meeting with a deliberate agenda — what the trusts hold, how distributions work, who the trustees are, what the foundation supports, how to think about wealth and responsibility. Catherine's stated preference is that this happens deliberately as the children enter adulthood, with cautious timing. We facilitate the design and the first 1-2 sessions; the family runs them after that. This is the governance scaffolding that makes the age-35 distribution rule actually work — it cannot work if the children show up at 35 with no context.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Run an age-scaled financial-literacy curriculum for each child.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Olivia (19) is ready for direct 1-on-1 conversations about how the trust architecture works and what her eventual role is. James (16) is ready for an intermediate version focused on the basics of how money, ownership, and obligations connect. Sophie (12) gets the introductory version — values, work, and the difference between earning and inheriting. PSA designs the curriculum and delivers it in coordination with you both. This is risk mitigation: the trusts hold real money, and an unprepared 35-year-old beneficiary is the failure mode this is designed to prevent.

closer_paragraph.label: What this means
closer_paragraph.body: The financial mechanics here — 529s, custodial Roths, the eventual 529-to-Roth rollover — produce real tax-advantaged compounding over the children's lifetimes. But the more important work in this section is the governance scaffolding: the values statement, the annual meeting, the literacy curriculum, the early conversation with Eleanor. Catherine has been explicit that philanthropy is how the children learn responsibility for wealth they did not create. That only happens if you build the structure around it now, before the transaction event makes the numbers much bigger and the stakes much higher.

sections.label: [PERSONAL — for owner(s)]
sections.intro_paragraph: Your charitable goals are specific and dollar-meaningful — Marcus's trade scholarships at North Cobb Tech, Catherine's children's-hospital board work, and a hard-rule preference for a family foundation post-liquidity rather than a perpetual DAF. The right architecture has three phases. Today through pre-LOI: a DAF for current giving and a pre-transaction gift of business interests at peak deductibility. Pre-transaction window: a Charitable Remainder Unitrust paired with a wealth-replacement structure so the family is not disinherited by the charitable gift. Post-liquidity: a family foundation that the children eventually help govern. Each piece is sequenced to capture value at the moment it is greatest.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Open and fund a Donor-Advised Fund this year with appreciated brokerage stock.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Select sponsor (Fidelity Charitable, Schwab Charitable, or a Georgia community foundation) and fund in the current tax year with $50K-$100K of appreciated taxable-account securities. At your combined federal+GA marginal rate of approximately 42.19%, the annual deduction is worth roughly $21,000-$42,000 in tax savings, with the midpoint $75K contribution producing about $28,000/year. Marcus and Catherine as primary advisors; children listed as successors. CPA tracks Form 8283, AGI limit, and any 5-year carryforward.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Gift 5% of non-voting Holdco units to the DAF before any binding LOI.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: The single highest-leverage charitable move in the entire plan. Pre-LOI gift of approximately 5% of post-recap non-voting Holdco units (with the valuation discount intact) captures both the income-tax deduction at FMV and avoidance of capital gains on the gifted portion at sale. Combined federal income tax + capital-gains avoided: approximately $900K-$2.4M depending on final HIS valuation ($32M-$48M range). Critical sequencing: gift must be completed BEFORE any binding sale agreement, otherwise the IRS treats it as an assignment of income and the deduction collapses. Coordinated with M&A counsel so the DAF appears as a shareholder of record in the data room.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Establish a $5M FLIP CRUT before the binding sale agreement.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: A Charitable Remainder Unitrust (FLIP variant designed to accept illiquid Holdco units, then convert to a unitrust on the triggering sale event) at $5M funding, 5% unitrust payout, 20-year+ horizon. Charitable deduction at funding: approximately $1.1M (range $1.0M-$1.5M) based on the present value of the remainder at the current §7520 rate of 5.00%. You receive lifetime income from the trust; the remainder passes to the named charity (DAF or eventual foundation). Pending decision: lifetime vs. term-of-years payout structure.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Pair the CRUT with a wealth-replacement ILIT funded by survivorship UL.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: The CRUT remainder goes to charity, which is the point — but it also means the family is partially disinherited from the gifted asset. The wealth-replacement structure pairs the CRUT with a survivorship UL policy inside an ILIT (the same ILIT from Section 10), so that the family receives an income-tax-free death benefit equal to or greater than the gifted value. Indicative scale: $5M of UL replacing a $5M CRUT contribution. Four inputs need to lock before binding: which appreciated asset funds the CRUT, the survivorship UL underwriting offer, the CRUT payout rate and term, and the ultimate charitable recipient (foundation vs. DAF vs. direct).

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Evaluate a 20-year zeroed-out grantor CLAT against the GRAT/IDGT alternatives.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: A Charitable Lead Annuity Trust pays an annuity stream to charity for 20 years, with the remainder passing to family at the end gift-tax-free. Highly sensitive to the §7520 rate (currently 5.00%) and to the funded asset's growth rate: at the §7520 hurdle, family remainder is approximately zero; at 8% growth, approximately $5M passes; at 10%, approximately $10M+. This is most powerful as a charitable+wealth-transfer combination, and overlaps mechanically with the GRAT and IDGT in Section 4. Counsel evaluates whether CLAT, GRAT, IDGT, or a stack of two is the right choice for your specific asset and rate environment.

recommendations_bullets.bold_imperative: Form the family foundation post-liquidity and grant the DAF balance into it.
recommendations_bullets.briefing: Post-transaction, form a Section 501(c)(3) private foundation with Marcus and Catherine as initial directors and the children added to the board on a phased timeline (Olivia first, then James, then Sophie as each enters adulthood). 5% minimum annual grantmaking. Initial funding scale and annual giving target are both set post-transaction once the actual proceeds are known. The DAF balance built up through the transaction window (current giving + the 5% Holdco gift) grants over to the foundation as the foundation comes online — mechanically simple and primarily a governance shift. CPA establishes Form 990-PF administration.

closer_paragraph.label: Why this sequence matters
closer_paragraph.body: The pre-LOI gift of Holdco units is the highest-leverage charitable move in the entire plan — approximately $900K-$2.4M of combined income-tax deduction and capital-gains avoidance — but only if it happens before a binding sale agreement exists. Miss that window and the IRS recharacterizes the gift as an assignment of income, the deduction collapses, and the capital gain comes back. The CRUT/ILIT pairing then handles the post-transaction wealth-replacement question so that the charitable commitment does not come at the family's expense. The foundation comes last, when scale is known and the children are old enough to be meaningful participants in governance — which is the part Catherine cares about most.

meeting_cadence_intro.intro_paragraph: A plan that is delivered and never revisited goes stale within 12 months. Here is how we will work together going forward. The cadence below is calibrated to the implementation intensity of the next 12 months, then settles into a steady-state rhythm tied to the transaction window. PSA quarterbacks the partners — you should not have to chase the estate attorney, the CPA, the M&A counsel, or the insurance broker; that coordination is our job.
immediate_next_steps: Review this document. Mark anything you want to discuss. Take your time — there is no "right answer" pace.
Decide on the top 3 priorities from the Executive Summary list. We will execute against those first.
Sign off on the Decisions Needed page so the estate attorney, CPA, and M&A counsel can begin specific drafting work.
Schedule the first implementation check-in for 30 days from delivery.
Agree on the introductory calls with the estate attorney and CPA candidates we are recommending.

entity.entity_type: LLC, taxed as S-Corporation (S-election filed at formation)
entity.industry_or_operations: 238220 — Plumbing, Heating, and Air-Conditioning Contractors. Specialty mechanical contractor (HVAC, process piping, controls) for data center, healthcare, and advanced manufacturing clients across the Southeast.

coverage_table.notes: 60% replacement, 24-month own-occ then any-occ; inadequate for HNW owner. No individual LTD. No coverage on Catherine. No business overhead expense policy.

$5M limit. Other business coverages: GL $2M occurrence/$4M aggregate; workers comp in place; commercial auto fleet in place; cyber $1M (likely under-insured given customer mix). No buy/sell life funding; no key person coverage on Derek or controller.

goals_priorities.intro_paragraph: These are the goals we identified together during discovery. Every recommendation in this plan ties back to one or more of them. We will revisit and refine these at every annual review.

goals.what_this_means_in_practice: Ranked goals: (1) Maximize after-tax sale proceeds — position HIS for a 3-5 year transaction window with structural and tax planning that compounds value retained; (2) Take care of Derek — treat his 12% fairly through any transition, both economically and as key continuity person; (3) Estate planning — reduce projected $11M+ estate-tax exposure through layered trusts, gifts, and life insurance; (4) Entity & real estate structure — separate Kennesaw real estate from operating company, build holding-company layer for future flexibility; (5) Tax strategy — capture federal benefit of GA PTET, optimize W-2 vs K-1 mix, evaluate cost segregation, §469 grouping, cash-balance plan; (6) Children's provision without demotivation — fund education and meaningful head start with controls preventing unrestricted access before age 35; (7) Catherine's real estate vision — build structural capacity for her to deploy meaningful capital into real estate development post-exit; (8) Risk management — match life, disability, and key-person coverage to actual exposures; (9) Personal investment diversification — build wealth outside the business, systematic taxable brokerage funding, asset location; (10) Philanthropic vehicles — DAF near-term, family foundation post-liquidity with children eventually involved in governance.

Marcus: trade scholarships at North Cobb Tech (his alma mater); values dignity of skilled trades work. Catherine: children's hospital where she serves on the board (community health access); sees philanthropy as how the children learn responsibility for wealth they did not create. Near-term: DAF. Post-liquidity: family foundation with children involved in governance (hard-rule preference, not just a DAF).

Children provided for but with restrictions — no unrestricted access to inherited wealth before age 35 (hard rule). Catherine wants children involved in foundation governance as values-formation mechanism. Family communication about wealth has been minimal: Olivia generally aware, James and Sophie not yet briefed; Catherine wants this to change deliberately as the children enter adulthood, with cautious timing. Anticipated future support of Marcus's mother Eleanor (assisted-living/in-home care within 5-10 years).

Treat Derek (12% owner) fairly through any transition — both as economic owner and as key continuity person; will not sell to a buyer who would relocate operations or eliminate Derek's role; intact business is non-negotiable (no strip-and-flip buyers). Derek's economic position has not been formalized in years and needs clarity. Marcus open to 1-3 years post-sale transition role. Employee communication is closed; only Marcus, Catherine, Derek, and PSA know.

Marcus values the dignity of skilled trades work — built HIS deliberately as a place where field people are paid and respected like the professionals they are. Wants to extend that through trade scholarships at North Cobb Tech. Catherine values community health access (her board work at the children's hospital) and sees philanthropy as how the children learn responsibility for wealth they did not create. Both deeply value the operating partner relationship with Derek; "taking care of Derek" is a values position, not just a planning priority.

implementation_roadmap.intro_paragraph: This is your project plan. It shows what gets done, when, and who owns it. We will update this together at every meeting.

rows.action: Form Holloway Properties LLC in Georgia and draft operating agreement.
Obtain Truist lender consent for transfer of Kennesaw HQ to common-ownership entity.
Confirm S-election integrity and shareholder eligibility (Marcus 88%, Derek 12%).
Engage specialist M&A counsel with documented F-reorg experience.

glossary.intro_paragraph: Plain-English definitions for the technical terms used in this plan.

entries.plain_english_definition: Allows up to 100% federal tax exclusion on the sale of qualified small business stock (QSBS) held more than 5 years, subject to limits. Generally requires C-Corp structure.
Generally disallows passive losses (like rental real estate) from offsetting active business income — but a grouping election can change that.
Disability insurance that reimburses the business for fixed operating costs while the owner is disabled.
Life insurance owned by the business on key employees, often used to informally fund deferred compensation arrangements.
A charitable account where you take the deduction at funding but recommend grants to charities over time. The simplest charitable vehicle.
Replaces a portion of income (typically 60–70%) if you cannot work due to illness or injury.
A trust that pays you back the full value of what you contribute (plus an IRS-set interest rate) over a set term. Any growth above that rate passes to your heirs free of gift tax.

A trust structured so the grantor pays the income tax on the trust's earnings (an additional gift-tax-free transfer) but the assets are outside the grantor's estate. "Defective" is a term of art — it works exactly as intended.
A trust that owns a life insurance policy so that proceeds pass outside the insured's taxable estate.
Non-binding outline of deal terms, typically signed before due diligence in a transaction.
An election that pays state tax at the entity level rather than the individual level — bypassing the $10,000 SALT cap on personal returns.
A non-qualified retirement plan funded by the employer for select key employees, typically with vesting schedules to encourage retention.

Estimates and projections shown in this plan reflect assumptions about future tax law, valuations, growth rates, and your circumstances. Actual results may vary materially. Specifically: (i) business valuation ranges are preliminary and not a formal valuation opinion; (ii) tax savings estimates are based on current law as of the plan date and your projected income; (iii) GRAT and IDGT projected wealth transfers depend on actual business performance versus the IRS-set hurdle rate during the trust term, and adverse performance can reduce or eliminate the planning benefit; (iv) insurance premium estimates are based on standard underwriting and may differ following actual underwriting.
